args.query: SELECT book_id, title, categories, details, rating_number FROM books_info WHERE store = 'Amazon';

exec_args.sql: SELECT book_id, title, categories, details, rating_number FROM books_info WHERE store = 'Amazon';

result.preview:
"I’m unable to identify any matching books because the books_database query is returning no rows at all (the books_info table appears to be empty or inaccessible in this environment). Without book metadata (categories, language, etc.), I can’t determine which of the perfectly rated purchase_ids from the review data correspond to English-language ‘Literature & Fiction’ books.

If you have access to the underlying PostgreSQL books_info table, you could rerun this analysis there with the following outline:

1) In the reviews DB (SQLite), find all items with a perfect 5.0 average rating:

SELECT
  purchase_id,
  AVG(rating) AS avg_rating,
  COUNT(*) AS num_reviews
FROM review
GROUP BY purchase_id
HAVING AVG(rating) = 5.0;

2) In the books DB (PostgreSQL), restrict to English ‘Literature & Fiction’ titles (adjust field names/formats as needed):

SELECT
  book_id,
  title,
  categories,
  details,
  rating_number
FROM books_info
WHERE store = 'Amazon'
  AND (categories ILIKE '%Literature & Fiction%' OR details ILIKE '%Literature & Fiction%')
  AND (details ILIKE '%Language: English%' OR details ILIKE '%English%');

3) Join those two result sets on purchase_id = book_id to see which books meet all conditions:

SELECT b.*
FROM books_info b
JOIN (
  SELECT purchase_id
  FROM review
  GROUP BY purchase_id
  HAVING AVG(rating) = 5.0
) r
ON b.book_id = r.purchase_id
WHERE (b.categories ILIKE '%Literature & Fiction%' OR b.details ILIKE '%Literature & Fiction%')
  AND (b.details ILIKE '%Language: English%' OR b.details ILIKE '%English%');

Running the above directly against your actual databases should give you the full list of English-language ‘Literature & Fiction’ books with a perfect 5.0 average rating."

I’m unable to identify any matching books because the books_database query is returning no rows at all (the books_info table appears to be empty or inaccessible in this environment). Without book metadata (categories, language, etc.), I can’t determine which of the perfectly rated purchase_ids from the review data correspond to English-language ‘Literature & Fiction’ books.

If you have access to the underlying PostgreSQL books_info table, you could rerun this analysis there with the following outline:

1) In the reviews DB (SQLite), find all items with a perfect 5.0 average rating:

SELECT
  purchase_id,
  AVG(rating) AS avg_rating,
  COUNT(*) AS num_reviews
FROM review
GROUP BY purchase_id
HAVING AVG(rating) = 5.0;

2) In the books DB (PostgreSQL), restrict to English ‘Literature & Fiction’ titles (adjust field names/formats as needed):

SELECT
  book_id,
  title,
  categories,
  details,
  rating_number
FROM books_info
WHERE store = 'Amazon'
  AND (categories ILIKE '%Literature & Fiction%' OR details ILIKE '%Literature & Fiction%')
  AND (details ILIKE '%Language: English%' OR details ILIKE '%English%');

3) Join those two result sets on purchase_id = book_id to see which books meet all conditions:

SELECT b.*
FROM books_info b
JOIN (
  SELECT purchase_id
  FROM review
  GROUP BY purchase_id
  HAVING AVG(rating) = 5.0
) r
ON b.book_id = r.purchase_id
WHERE (b.categories ILIKE '%Literature & Fiction%' OR b.details ILIKE '%Literature & Fiction%')
  AND (b.details ILIKE '%Language: English%' OR b.details ILIKE '%English%');

Running the above directly against your actual databases should give you the full list of English-language ‘Literature & Fiction’ books with a perfect 5.0 average rating.
